finding_results.evidence: Combined instances reduced from 15+ to 4. Remaining instances: (1) chapters/chapter_08.md:7 'the kind of person who talked with his hands' (natural wondering), (2) chapters/chapter_08.md:29 'the kind of person who Googled that' (free indirect thought), (3) chapters/chapter_08.md:121 'The specific, stubborn pride of a person who would rather absorb the blow' (structural), (4) chapters/chapter_08.md:211 closing sentence (structural). No two consecutive paragraphs use either formula. Replaced instances use varied strategies: action beat (chapters/chapter_08.md:43 'hard enough to rattle her pen cup'), embedded physical action (chapters/chapter_08.md:49 'kept talking before she'd finished chewing'), compressed declarative (chapters/chapter_08.md:77 'let the pressure say what she didn't'; chapters/chapter_08.md:141 'more weight than help, more monument than gift'; chapters/chapter_08.md:141 'Two taps and a thumb scan. Four hundred dollars, gone.'), sensory detail (chapters/chapter_08.md:85 'without knowing she was doing it — unguarded, unconscious'; chapters/chapter_08.md:89 'pressing her into the couch cushions with an insistence that somehow felt like being held'), free indirect thought (chapters/chapter_08.md:147 'Not abstractly good — specifically, physically good').
finding_results.notes: 11 instances replaced using at least 5 distinct syntactic strategies. 4 retained where the formula does active structural or psychological work. Well under the 6-instance ceiling.

finding_results.evidence: chapters/chapter_08.md:67 — 'an ex-husband who'd started a joint Instagram with his new girlfriend captioned things like *Sunday meal prep with my partner in crime*'. Zero content overlap with any Margo example_line in the style bible. No reference to hot yoga, posting habits (the detail is about a joint account and captioning, not individual posting), or other calibration sample elements. Detail is original, specific, comic (the 'partner in crime' caption is cringe-specific), and performs the same character work: Margo's dry awareness of her ex-husband's conspicuous performed-happiness online.
finding_results.notes: Replacement detail sits naturally in the existing sentence structure ('an ex-husband who [X], and a bullshit detector that ran at an industrial frequency').

finding_results.evidence: Three discrete dual-weight moments in scene 4: (1) chapters/chapter_08.md:169 — phone notification: Simone's eyes flick to the screen and back in under a second (existing, preserved). (2) chapters/chapter_08.md:197 — physical beat: 'something in her face went still — not cold, but focused, as if she were choosing her next words carefully' — reads as sincerity from Nora's angle, as calculation from the reader's. (3) chapters/chapter_08.md:197 — dialogue: 'You know why it worked, right? The fact that he didn't ask. If he'd come out and said *can you Venmo me*, you'd have hesitated. But the pride, the *I'll figure it out* — that's what got you. Because you can't stand watching someone carry something alone when you could lighten it.' Simone is describing the mechanism she engineered with uncanny precision. From Nora's perspective, deep emotional intelligence. From the reader's, Simone narrating her own con. The word 'worked' carries dual meaning: emotionally resonated / operationally succeeded. Moments 2 and 3 occur immediately adjacent to the key validation lines ('It's not stupid. It's generous. That's who you are.').
finding_results.notes: All three moments are grounded in physical detail (phone flick, face going still) or dialogue rhythm (too-perfect analysis of the not-asking mechanism), not authorial editorializing.

finding_results.evidence: Comic escalation across four scenes: Scene 1 (chapters/chapter_08.md:3-39) — warm observational humor baseline (cerulean orbs, startled deer, badly lit mammals). Scene 2 (chapters/chapter_08.md:43-85) — sharper: Margo's 'He's not even in the same area code as Camus' and 'I have to write a straight-faced pass on this bullshit by Friday' (chapters/chapter_08.md:49) hit harder than scene 1's gentle comedy; Margo chewing before finishing talking extends the comic characterization. Scene 3 (chapters/chapter_08.md:89-153) — absurdist: 'she keeps telling me it was a satin ribbon, like the thread count is relevant to the cat's intestinal situation' (chapters/chapter_08.md:103); the image of a woman insisting on ribbon quality while her cat is in surgery is genuine absurdist comedy. Scene 4 (chapters/chapter_08.md:157-211) — dark comedy peak: Simone saying 'You know why it worked, right? The fact that he didn't ask' (chapters/chapter_08.md:197) while describing the exact trap she set; simultaneously tender (Simone knows Nora perfectly) and darkly funny (Simone is admiring her own mechanism). The reader laughs and the laugh curdles.
finding_results.notes: Comedy builds from observational (scene 1) → cutting/profane (scene 2) → absurdist (scene 3) → dark/ironic (scene 4). Each register is sharper than the previous.

finding_results.evidence: Three instances of natural profanity across three characters: (1) Margo: 'I have to write a straight-faced pass on this bullshit by Friday' (chapters/chapter_08.md:49) — casual workplace frustration, consistent with moderate-to-high profile. (2) Nora: '"Shit." She said it out loud, to her empty apartment.' (chapters/chapter_08.md:105) — gut reaction to vet emergency, consistent with moderate profile (mostly 'shit' and 'fuck' when surprised). (3) Simone: 'She went through some real shit with her marriage' (chapters/chapter_08.md:173) — casual conversational deployment, consistent with liberal profile. Each instance sits naturally in its surrounding dialogue or interiority. None reads as forced or performatively edgy.
finding_results.notes: Profanity distribution matches style-bible profiles. Margo's is embedded in a comic rant (character-true). Nora's is a reflexive exclamation to an empty room. Simone's is casual and analytical — profanity as punctuation in a fair-minded assessment.

finding_results.evidence: Editing metaphors in chapter reduced from 3 to 1 (in dialogue). (1) REPLACED: chapters/chapter_08.md:29 — 'she'd written margin notes like *would your protagonist really not notice this?*' replaced with 'she'd wanted to reach through the page and shake every single one of them' (physical impulse, not editorial vocabulary). (2) REPLACED: chapters/chapter_08.md:79 — 'her entire professional existence was built on the ability to see what a story was doing underneath what it said it was doing' replaced with 'her entire professional existence was built on catching inconsistencies — she saw what didn't add up, she noticed the gaps between what people presented and what they meant' (non-editorial vocabulary: inconsistencies, gaps, presented, meant). (3) PRESERVED: chapters/chapter_08.md:183 — 'It's like reading a manuscript where every page is brilliant but you can't see the author photo' (dialogue, comic, earns its place). Zero editing metaphors remain in narration. Total editing metaphors: 1 (in dialogue).
finding_results.notes: The replacement at line 29 uses a visceral physical image. The replacement at line 79 uses pattern-recognition and deception-detection language without editorial/literary vocabulary. The preserved dialogue metaphor at line 183 remains comic and character-true.
